items.q: What happens at a constructive (divergent) plate boundary?
items.correct: Plates move apart, magma rises to fill the gap and solidifies, forming new oceanic crust — for example at the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.
items.misconception: At constructive boundaries the plates collide and build mountains.
items.random_wrong: At constructive boundaries the plates slide past each other sideways.
items.why: Students confuse 'constructive' with 'collision'. Constructive means new crust is constructed; the plates move apart, not together. Sliding sideways is a conservative/transform boundary.

items.q: Why is an ageing population a challenge for HICs?
items.correct: Rising dependency ratio means fewer workers support more pensioners through tax, increasing pressure on pensions, healthcare and social care services.
items.misconception: Because old people stop contributing anything to society.
items.random_wrong: Because old people consume too much food.
items.why: The issue is structural — the ratio of workers to dependants, not the worth of older people. Students moralise where the geographical concern is about funding flows.

items.q: What is the ITCZ and why does it bring rain?
items.correct: The Intertropical Convergence Zone is where trade winds from both hemispheres converge near the equator, forcing air to rise, cool and produce heavy convectional rainfall.
items.misconception: The ITCZ is a fixed band of permanent storms at the equator.
items.random_wrong: The ITCZ is the boundary between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
items.why: The ITCZ shifts seasonally north and south with the sun, driving monsoon rains. Students treat it as static — its movement explains why tropical regions have wet/dry seasons.
It's Crutzen and Stoermer. “Lovelock and Margulis” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many planetary boundaries did Rockstrom et al identify in 2009?
items.why: It's 9. “12” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of global internet traffic uses submarine cables?
items.why: The real figure is 99 percent. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
